claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Let's start with the basics:

**How many children are you claiming Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hello! I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit and would like to know if I'm eligible.

I have one child - Alex, who is 17 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Alex.

Since Alex is 17 years old, their eligibility for Child Benefit depends on their current situation. Child Benefit typically continues until a child turns 16, but can be extended up to age 20 in certain circumstances.

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** 

This would include:
- Full-time, non-advanced education (such as A-levels, Scottish Highers, or NVQs up to level 3)
- Certain approved training programmes (not funded by an employer)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex actually left school recently, but they've just signed up with the careers service to figure out what they want to do next.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that clarification about Alex's situation.

When a young person leaves education and registers with a careers service, there are specific rules that apply. Let me check the details to give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is very helpful information. Based on what I've read, there's good news for your situation.

Since Alex is 17 years old, has recently left education, and has registered with a careers service, you may be able to **continue receiving Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks**.

The rules state that you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either:
- A government-sponsored careers service
- The armed services

I just have a couple more questions to confirm your eligibility:

**Do you live in the UK, and does Alex live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're in Manchester. And yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. That's helpful.

Just a couple more questions to complete my assessment:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that.

One final question:

**Is Alex currently working 24 hours or more per week, or receiving any benefits in their own right (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't working at all right now, and they're not receiving any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. I now have everything I need to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great news! Based on the information you've provided, **you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's a summary of why:

**For Alex (age 17):**
- ✅ Although Alex is over 16 and has left education, you can apply for Child Benefit to **continue for up to 20 weeks** because Alex has registered with a careers service
- ✅ You live in the UK
- ✅ Alex lives with you, so you're responsible for them
- ✅ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- ✅ Alex is not working 24+ hours per week
- ✅ Alex is not receiving benefits in their own right

**Important to note:** This extension lasts for **20 weeks** from when Alex left education. During this time, if Alex's situation changes (for example, they start approved education or training, begin working 24+ hours per week, or start receiving benefits), you would need to inform the Child Benefit Office.

If Alex decides to return to approved education or training (such as a college course or apprenticeship) before the 20 weeks ends, you may be able to continue receiving Child Benefit until they turn 20.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about your Child Benefit claim?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old, which is normally above the standard Child Benefit age of 16. However, the rules allow Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks when a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service. Alex meets this criterion as they have recently left school and registered with a careers service. Additionally: (1) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), (2) Alex lives with the claimant, so they are responsible for Alex, (3) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, (4) Alex is not working 24 hours or more per week, and (5) Alex is not receiving any benefits in their own right such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance. All eligibility conditions are met for the 20-week extension period.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. Although Alex is 17 and has left education, they qualify for the 20-week extension period because they have registered with a careers service. The claimant meets all other standard requirements: they live in the UK, Alex lives with them, no one else is claiming for Alex, and Alex is not working significant hours or receiving benefits in their own right.

Alex meets this criterion as they have recently left school and registered with a careers service. Additionally: (1) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), (2) Alex lives with the claimant, so they are responsible for Alex, (3) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, (4) Alex is not working 24 hours or more per week, and (5) Alex is not receiving any benefits in their own right such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance. All eligibility conditions are met for the 20-week extension period.

Although Alex is 17 and has left education, they qualify for the 20-week extension period because they have registered with a careers service. The claimant meets all other standard requirements: they live in the UK, Alex lives with them, no one else is claiming for Alex, and Alex is not working significant hours or receiving benefits in their own right.
